1: A theme: Hiram king of Tyre, a Gentile ruler, immediately recognizes Solomon's anointing and sends servants to honor him. A Gentile king's response to the anointed Davidic king anticipates the Gentile recognition of Christ: the magi from the east (Matthew 2:1-2), the Roman centurion ('truly this man was the Son of God,' Mark 15:39), and ultimately the nations' incorporation into the covenant people. The Gentile recognition of God's anointed is a recurrent pattern that Christ fulfills universally.
2: A theme: Solomon's message to Hiram begins with a statement of purpose — to build the temple. The communication of kingdom purpose to a Gentile ally is the OT analog of the NT's proclamation of the gospel to the nations: what God is doing in and through his anointed king is announced publicly, and Gentile cooperation is invited. The church's mission, like Solomon's letter to Hiram, announces what God is building and invites the nations to participate.
3: A shadow: 'My father David could not build a house for the name of the LORD his God because of the wars surrounding him on every side.' David's inability to build — not for lack of desire but because the time was not right — is the OT analog of Christ's words that 'the time is not yet' (John 7:6, 8). The temple project requires rest from enemies as its prerequisite. The NT's temple — Christ's body, the church — is built in the eschatological peace secured by Christ's defeat of the powers (Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 2:14-16).
4: A shadow: 'But now the LORD my God has given me rest on every side. There is no enemy or threat of disaster.' The Solomonic rest is the OT's fullest realization of the land-rest promised in Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 12:9-10). The author of Hebrews uses this rest language to point beyond Solomonic history to the eschatological rest that 'remains for the people of God' (Hebrews 4:9): 'For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on' (Hebrews 4:8). Solomon's rest is real but partial — a chapter, not the conclusion, in the narrative of God's rest with his people.
5: A type: 'I therefore intend to build a house for the name of the LORD my God, as the LORD told my father David: Your son, whom I will set on your throne in your place, shall build the house for my name.' The Nathan oracle (2 Samuel 7:12-13) promised that David's son would build the temple — a promise that has an immediate fulfillment (Solomon builds the stone temple) and an ultimate fulfillment (Christ is the true temple). John 2:19-21 makes the identification explicit: when Jesus says 'destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up,' John explains 'he was speaking about the temple of his body.' Solomon builds the type; Christ is the antitype.
6: A theme: Solomon requests cedars of Lebanon from Hiram, and proposes that Israelite workers will work alongside Hiram's servants. The collaboration of Israel and Gentile Tyre in building the temple is a shadow of the church — Jew and Gentile working together to build up the body of Christ. Paul's language in Ephesians 2:19-22 is explicitly temple-building language: the church, built on the foundation of apostles and prophets, with Christ as the cornerstone, grows 'into a holy temple in the Lord.'
7: A revelation of God: Hiram's response — 'Blessed be the LORD today, who has given David a wise son to rule over this great people' — is the blessing of YHWH by a Gentile king. This is the Abrahamic promise in miniature: 'through you all the families of the earth shall be blessed' (Genesis 12:3), and Gentiles will bless YHWH. The NT completion is the universal praise of God among the nations (Romans 15:9-11; Revelation 5:13): every tongue confessing that Jesus Christ is Lord to the glory of God the Father (Philippians 2:11).
8: A theme: Hiram's agreement to supply cedar and cypress timber in exchange for Solomon's payment. The economic exchange — Israel's agricultural produce for Lebanon's timber — is the practical machinery of God's purpose to have his temple built. Providence works through ordinary commerce and international relations. The NT makes the same point about the church's mission: God works through tent-makers (Acts 18:3) and financial supporters (Philippians 4:15-16) to advance the construction of the living temple.
9: A theme: the logistics of timber — floated down by sea, then handed off to Solomon's workers — demonstrate that the temple's construction requires careful coordination across great distances. The church's construction is similarly a multi-party, multi-location project: apostles lay foundations in one city, workers water what others plant (1 Corinthians 3:6), and the Spirit coordinates the whole. The Solomonic logistics are a shadow of the Spirit's coordination of the worldwide building project of the body of Christ.
10: A theme: 'So Hiram supplied Solomon with all the cedar and cypress timber he wanted.' Complete provision from a Gentile source for the holy project. The theme of sufficiency — YHWH ensuring that all necessary materials come together for the temple's completion — anticipates the NT's confidence that God who began the good work will bring it to completion (Philippians 1:6). The temple project never lacks cedar; the church's building project will not lack what the Spirit supplies.
11: A theme: Solomon's payment to Hiram — twenty thousand cors of wheat and twenty thousand cors of pure olive oil — is a massive annual provision. The covenant exchange between Solomon and Hiram (grain and oil for timber) mirrors the covenant exchange in the gospel: what Israel has (the oracles of God, the covenant promises, the Messiah) goes out to the Gentiles; what the Gentiles bring (the fullness of the nations, the riches of the nations) comes in. Paul describes this exchange in Romans 15:27: 'if the Gentiles have come to share in their spiritual blessings, they ought also to be of service to them in material blessings.'
12: A revelation of God: 'The LORD gave Solomon wisdom as he had promised. There was peace between Hiram and Solomon, and the two of them made a treaty.' YHWH's faithfulness to his promise and the resulting peace between Israel and Tyre is the pattern that the NT presents as the gospel's outcome: the peace that Christ makes between Jew and Gentile through his cross (Ephesians 2:14-16). Solomon's covenant-peace with Hiram is the OT form; Christ's atonement is the cosmic content.
13: A shadow: 'King Solomon conscripted forced labor from all Israel — thirty thousand men in all.' The shadow side of Solomon's magnificent achievement is the conscription of forced labor. This is the beginning of the burden that will fracture the kingdom (1 Kings 12:4). Christ's 'conscription' of disciples is radically different: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light' (Matthew 11:28-30). The greater Son of David replaces conscription with invitation, coercion with rest.
14: A theme: the rotation of workers — one month in Lebanon, two months at home — suggests a system designed to prevent the permanent displacement of workers from their families and farms. Even within the conscription system, there is a pastoral concern for the workers' welfare. The NT's leadership principle similarly involves care for those who serve: elders are to tend the flock 'not domineering over those in your charge, but being examples to the flock' (1 Peter 5:3).
15: A theme: seventy thousand carriers and eighty thousand stonecutters in the hill country — the scale of human labor required to build the temple is immense. The temple is built at great human cost. The NT's temple — the church — is built at even greater cost: 'Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her' (Ephesians 5:25). The massive labor investment in Solomon's temple is a shadow of the infinite cost of the living temple, purchased not with human labor but with the blood of the Son (Acts 20:28).
16: A theme: three thousand three hundred supervisors who oversaw the workers — the managerial layer of the Solomonic building project. The church similarly has a structure of oversight: 'God has appointed in the church first apostles, second prophets, third teachers...' (1 Corinthians 12:28). The Solomonic administrative hierarchy for temple construction is the OT shadow of the spiritual gifts and offices through which the Spirit builds the living temple.
17: A shadow: 'At the king's command, they quarried large, expensive stones to lay the foundation of the house with cut stone.' The foundation of the Solomonic temple was laid with enormous, precisely cut stones — the costly, careful work of preparation before the visible structure could rise. The NT's temple-building language is consistent: 'no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ' (1 Corinthians 3:11); the apostles and prophets are 'the foundation' with Christ as the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20). Solomon's cut-stone foundation is the shadow; Christ as foundation is the reality.
18: A shadow: 'Solomon's builders, Hiram's builders, and the men of Gebal cut and prepared the timber and stone for the building of the house.' The temple's construction required the coordinated labor of Israelites and Gentiles working together. This inter-ethnic building project is the OT's most concrete image of what Paul describes in Ephesians 2:11-22: the wall between Jew and Gentile broken down in Christ, 'built together into a dwelling place for God by the Spirit.' Solomon's temple was built by Jews and Gentiles for a stone house; Christ's temple is built by Jews and Gentiles (and every nation) for the living dwelling of God in the Spirit.
